role_summary: Sasser, Georgia native; participant in the early-1960s Southwest Georgia Movement organized by Rev. Charles Sherrod (catalog entry 18) and the Albany-Movement-affiliated SNCC field network in Worth, Terrell, and Sumter Counties.
ai_reading: Browner's neighbor counts are moderate (peak 78), with Johnnie Ruth McCullar (entry 65) as the top match and the broader cluster including Juadine Henderson (entry 68), Vernon Dahmer Jr. (entry 121), and Charles McLaurin (entry 17). The novel observation his page surfaces is the Southwest-Georgia-cohort signature: Browner, Robert McClary (catalog entry 109), and the joint Charles Sherrod (entry 18) and William G. Anderson (entry 126) interviews document the same Sherrod-led SNCC organizing tradition that anchored the Albany Movement; the catalog's clustering of these Southwest Georgia voices suggests the embedding reads the Worth-Terrell-Sumter County rural-organizing register as a coherent vector signature distinct from the more-photographed Mississippi-and-Alabama field-organizer cohorts.
biographical_paragraph: While Browner's LoC oral history carries a publication-block audit tier (known issues prevent full publication-grade certification, and any specific quoted claim drawn from the transcript itself should be confirmed against external sources before citation), the external biographical record is documented through the Civil Rights History Project finding aid for entry 23 [src: 1]. Browner grew up in Sasser, Georgia (Terrell County, in the Southwest Georgia rural-cotton belt) and came of age in the early 1960s when the SNCC Southwest Georgia Project, led by Rev. Charles Sherrod (catalog entry 18) and based in Albany, was organizing voter registration and mass meetings across the surrounding counties [src: 1]. His oral history conducted by Hasan Kwame Jeffries in Albany in March 2013 documents how he came to be involved in the Southwest Georgia Movement and his organizing work alongside the SNCC field staff of that project.

sources.title: Clifford D. Browner Jr. oral history
sources.publisher: Civil Rights History Project, Library of Congress (AFC 2010/039)
